item.title: Re: The island of stability
item.content:encoded: The ting is, no one knows until the guys at CERN make these elements. maybe it doesn't even exsist. maybe the elements will be as stable a rock, maybe they will have half lives of days. no one knows, but my money's on 'stable as a rock'

item.title: Re: The island of stability
item.content:encoded: "Island of Stability" means that they will be much more stable than an element as heavy as the ones we've seen so far (Ununoctium, anyone?) would be. This doesn't mean half-lives of a million or so years, but closer to hours or days, compared to the expected milli- or micro- seconds.

item.title: The island of stability
item.content:encoded: Umm… i'm pretty sure that eventually it will become stable. there's a theoretical 'island of stability', where elements such as element 126 will be stable, or only decay over millions of years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see the Foundation as a little like the military, in that you do what you are told and don't complain about it.
You get a memo that you have been re-assigned to 1885 and ordering you to report to the dentist. Nobody ever asks your opinion or preference. Sure, you can request a different assignment, but the powers that be tend to make sure that those reassignmetns are far worse than what you wanted to avoid. In the military, that means that instead of digging latrines you empty them. In the Foundation that means that instead of having your teeth taken out by a licensed professional using anesthesia, they assign you to the "tooth fairy" and let you do it yourself with your bare hands. Or, if they don't like you, your the new guard for the "old man".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it weren't for the final note (which reads a bit over-the-top to me, but it gets the point across), I'd agree. Given the small but far-from-nonzero chance that the next tooth converted will turn into a micro-nuke, though (and dear god, what if the fallout all decays without diminishing, like the core does?!), I can understand the call for extreme measures. I'm so-so on the effectiveness of the execution, but I can see where they're coming from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't the false tooth have the protective covering, or did it not like the others. If so, it could take some time to determine the issue existed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All of which are vastly inferior to the real thing. Crippling may be an overstatement, but the point stands. It would really, really suck. Considering how dubious the research prospects are with any SCP (when magic is a plausible cause, science may not always produce results), there's no way people would be willing to submit to this without some massive compensation. Khym's suggestion is much more reasonable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had actually thought about something similar to this, but discarded it during my touch-ups.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see your concern, but I'm a little confused as to why you're saying that losing all your teeth is crippling. Disfiguring? Sure. But crippling? Not really. And even then, there are these wonderful things called "dentures" and "dental implants".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's still an issue. Seriously, people would not be willing to work on this. I mean, a keter class that could break out and kill you, that's one thing. But something where you're absolutely guaranteed to be permanently crippled "for your own safety"? No one would do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Following Incident 1885-A, personnel who are fully edentulous are preferred to staff the facility containing SCP-1885 and all researchers assigned to SCP-1885 are to have all their teeth extracted prior to being transferred to the site.

That seems way over-the-top. A more reasonable procedure would be to designate a "red-zone" around the SCP, and anyone who enters the red-zone must, right before exiting it, stick a Geiger-counter in their mouth, and if it shows they have a radioactive tooth then they have to stay in the red-zone until a Foundation surgeon comes to remove it. And have all the researchers stick a Geiger-counter in their mouths once a day, even if they haven't entered the red-zone that day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hence the unofficial nickname for the staff at that site: "The Gummy Bears"

They're a little sensitive about that, though, so don't use it in front of them unless you want to lose your own teeth in a more… agressive manner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But at least his family got a very nice condolence card and fruit basket from the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good rewrite, it makes me happy to see something with potential be rescued. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, except that I can't understand why the containment procedures fail to include a warning sign reading, "You can't handle the tooth."

Because the man who suggested it was shot, dragged through the streets, shot again, buried, exhumed, set on fire, shot one last time, and then launched into the sun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A definite improvement over the first version. That said…

Following Incident 1885-A, personnel who are fully edentulous are preferred to staff the facility containing SCP-1885 and all researchers assigned to SCP-1885 are to have all their teeth extracted prior to being transferred to the site.

There had better be a hell of a good compensation for this, or else you will get literally no one willing to work with this item. If nothing else, I could see exposure to SCP-427 being permitted so a one-year assignment to this SCP doesn't cripple you for the rest of your life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know that a chunk of radioactive matter by itself, especially one this small, isn't sufficient to make a nuclear warhead. But two points: first, that memo was written by a dying man who was trying to impress the importance of monitoring this upon those who would come after him; he's allowed to be a little dramatic. Second, when this thing starts producing elements in the range of 130 or higher, who knows what amount will be sufficient to fuck shit up? Even if it's just a small area (a town or even just a city block), there would likely be a shit-ton of radiation contamination, plus having to explain/cover up how a pinpoint nuke got set off.

"next higher element" is a little awkward

Agreed. I wasn't sure how to word that better, but your suggestions work. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sort of mindless filling needs to be rooted out as soon as possible; this is a place to chew over an SCP, not jaw away like this… I mean, what did you expect- a plaque?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, the rewrite of the radioactive tooth. A definite improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe "element of next highest atomic number?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A chunk of radioactive matter does not a warhead make…

Also: "next higher element" is a little awkward; perhaps "next element on the periodic table" or "next element in sequence" or some such?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted, and well done on turning a dubious SCP into a good one.

Well, except that I can't understand why the containment procedures fail to include a warning sign reading, "You can't handle the tooth."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And thats the tooth

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gum on guys. I don't know if this needs to be drilled in, but pun threads are not a forum's crowning moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if I'm biting off more than I can chew with this one, but that tooth is pretty RAD-icle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The wisdom of that joke fills a cavity in my heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, cool, thanks for the answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there was more of it, maybe. But it's an incredibly small amount (less than a couple of mL) and anything they take out of it stops acting anomalously. At its current composition, there's not enough to do much more than give you cancer with long term exposure. But who knows what'll happen once you start getting elements that are currently purely theoretical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, question though: I don't know how radiation works but couldn't we use this for power or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish I knew more teeth-related puns…

Oh wait, here's one;

Way to make a mountain out of a MOLAR hill!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was a memo written by a dying man to those who would come after him. He wanted to be emphatic in getting his point across. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm…I like it. But is there anything special with the last note, or are some words just randomly bolded?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rewrite/retake on a nuclear tooth posted by AvengR last week and deleted this past weekend.
